title: Is a wealth tax actually possible?

discourse_skeleton.overall_topic: Feasibility and proper locus of designing and implementing a wealth tax

major_stasis_points.question_at_issue: Whose responsibility is it to design and advance wealth tax policy?
claims: Correct implementation of tax policy is the job of experts.
Policy design should not rest on a single individual, however intelligent.
It is not the job of campaigners/private actors like Gary and collaborators to do the detailed policy design work.
antitheses: Advocates of a policy bear the burden of producing its detailed implementation design.
warrants: Tax policy design requires institutional expertise and capacity that resides in governments, civil services, and think tanks.
Distributing design across institutions reduces error relative to reliance on any individual.

major_stasis_points.question_at_issue: Can the UK feasibly implement a wealth tax unilaterally?
claims: A wealth tax would be especially difficult for the UK to implement on its own.
Wealth inequality is not a UK-specific problem, so action need not (and arguably should not) be UK-only.
International tax coordination is feasible, as shown by the international minimum corporation tax.
antitheses: The UK could and should implement a wealth tax unilaterally regardless of international coordination.
warrants: Capital and wealth mobility make unilateral wealth taxation vulnerable to avoidance.
Existing international tax agreements demonstrate that multilateral coordination on taxing wealth/capital is achievable.

facts_to_verify: Whether the UK faces distinctive structural obstacles to implementing a wealth tax compared to other countries.
Whether wealth inequality of comparable magnitude exists in countries beyond the UK.
Whether an international minimum corporation tax currently exists and is in force.
discourse_skeleton.meta_comment: Monologic source: Gary's argument shifts the burden of detailed policy design away from himself toward institutional actors, while using the international corporation tax precedent to argue that the right scale for a wealth tax is multilateral, not UK-unilateral. No interlocutor present, so 'agreement' fields are not meaningfully applicable.
